How I Think

The thinking behind the work

01
I go upstream first

Most briefs present a symptom as the problem. A brand that cannot grow its audience does not have a content problem — it usually has a positioning problem. I do not stop at the first explanation that makes sense. I stay with the question until something truer begins to show itself.

02
Brand strategy is a sales problem

Brands that have not solved their strategy problem keep spending on advertising indefinitely and can never stop. The spend becomes a substitute for clarity. Brand strategy, done properly, makes a brand strong enough to be remembered without constantly shouting.

03
I ride the waves

Strategy is always moving. I maintain a deliberate practice of staying close to the conditions that shape how brands need to think — business climates, cultural shifts, AI reports, market movements. I want to be immediately relevant for a brand trying to crack tomorrow's world.

04
I prove the thinking by building

I built City on a Hill — a youth development community — from zero with no ad spend, no existing audience, and no safety net. Just brand strategy, positioning, and the discipline to sustain it. It now reaches over 1,000 young people across Nigeria and the UK.

Selected Work

Strategy that thinks

FMCG · Nigeria Market Entry

Brand & Market Entry Strategy

Sankofa Natural Spices

"How do you enter a market as the healthier choice without making health the reason to choose you?"

“Crown Your Pot.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

Sankofa Natural Spices wanted to enter Nigeria and compete with seasoning giants — positioning as the first mass-market, non-MSG, no-salt, healthy spice option in the market.

The Challenge

Jollof wars between Nigeria and Ghana meant a Ghanaian brand entering Nigeria carried immediate cultural risk. Nigerians have intensely loyal taste palates — Knorr, Maggi, and Terra have decades of deep penetration. How does a new, healthier option earn genuine consideration without triggering defensiveness or coming across as clinical?

My Approach

Sankofa could not arrive waving a Ghanaian flag — that is an Achilles heel, not a differentiator. It could not lead with health as a banner either — Nigerians do not want to be told their cooking is wrong. Through research, I found the real emotional truth: Nigerians want guilt reduction, not guilt elimination. So Sankofa enters not as a replacement but as the brand that completes the pot — meeting Nigerians exactly where they are, respecting their cultural truth, and offering something that suits both ego and health at the same time.

The Big Idea

Crown Your Pot. Not "switch your seasoning" — crown it. Elevate what you already cook. Sankofa is not asking Nigerians to change how they cook. It is asking them to add one thing: a seasoning that is completely on their side. Functionally, it adds to what you already cook. Emotionally, your cooking deserves better — not different, better.

Fashion · Pan-African

Brand Strategy & Business Model

INTERSECT

"How do you build a pan-African fashion brand that is culturally authentic, functionally accessible, and economically scalable — all at once?"

“Wear Your Continent.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

INTERSECT needed a brand and business strategy that could make pan-African cultural fashion viable — not just beautiful. The brand connects artisans across the continent to the women who wear their stories, through fashion.

The Challenge

Cultural differences still stand and people's purses still speak. Premium African fashion is either too runway-ready or too expensive for everyday use. It is more expensive to import from Kenya to Nigeria than from China to Nigeria — a perplexing reality that keeps African craft inaccessible to the very consumers who should own it.

My Approach

I designed both the business strategy and the brand strategy simultaneously. The solution was a two-product architecture: INTERSECT Original, fashion-forward and imported from Kenyan artisans as pure cultural statements; and INTERSECT Functional, made in Nigeria to meet practical everyday standards. On the brand side, I wove in the narratives of the artisans themselves — making the brand feel genuinely pan-African and positioning it as a proof of concept for scalable economic collaboration across the continent.

The Big Idea

Wear Your Continent. Not a slogan about fashion — a statement about identity, pride, and the quiet economic revolution that happens when African women choose African craft. Every piece is a vote for a world where the roads between us are shorter than they told us.

Edtech · Black Women Global

Brand Strategy & Launch Direction

Oduko

"How do you build a brand that serves two very different women — the one who needs to grow and the one who teaches growth — without confusing either of them?"

“A Space With a Soul.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

Oduko is a web app designed for the holistic upskilling of Black women globally — built across six development pathways. The founder wanted to speak to two audiences simultaneously and communicate all six pillars from the very beginning.

The Challenge

A new brand cannot afford to divide the minds of the people it is trying to reach. Launching with two audiences and six value propositions at once creates a brand that feels like everything and lands as nothing. The harder truth: a tech product will not earn trust without first earning belonging. The product needed a soul before it needed a feature list.

My Approach

I recommended that Oduko enter the market not as a tech product but as a space with a soul — a women's community you want to belong to before you ever know it is an app. Sell the feeling first. Earn the trust. Then reveal the infrastructure. The brand DNA I developed defined Oduko's tone as pop-culture fluent and community-driven — so that learning never feels clinical, and growth never feels like homework.

The Big Idea

A Space With a Soul. Before Oduko is a platform, it is a place. Before it is a product, it is a feeling of being seen, challenged, and celebrated by women who understand exactly where you are. The six pillars exist — but they are revealed through relationship, not through a features page.

Fintech · B2B · Intra-African Trade

Brand & Campaign Strategy · Full Market Playbook

Brydge Finance

"How do you build trust for a digital-first B2B platform in markets where trade is still built on handshakes, local language, and face-to-face relationships?"

“Your Trade Padi.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

Brydge Finance is a B2B platform simplifying intra-African trade — offering payments, compliance, trade finance, and market penetration services for importers and exporters across Nigeria, Cameroon, Ivory Coast, Benin, Togo, and Kenya.

The Challenge

Most African businesses still import from China. Intra-African trade feels risky and unfamiliar. Brydge's real competitors were not FrontEdge or Cellulant — they were habit, informal networks, and fear of fraud. The target audience was also three distinct psychographic groups — importers, exporters, and financiers — each with entirely different pain points and trust triggers.

My Approach

I built a full market playbook with three distinct audience personas and separate messaging ladders for each, phased across three campaign stages. The multilingual dimension was non-negotiable: Hausa for Kano, French for Francophone West Africa. The brand line — Your Trade Padi — humanised a complex B2B platform into a relationship, translated across five languages so every market heard it in their own voice.

The Big Idea

Your Trade Padi. Not "your trade platform" — your Padi. In Nigerian vernacular, a padi is someone you trust, someone who shows up when the deal gets complicated. Brydge positions itself not as infrastructure but as the most reliable relationship in the room — translated across Ore, Oyim, Aboki, Pote, and Budda.

Fintech · Digital Bullion · Asset Investment

Integrated Media Strategy · Brand Positioning

SANU Coin

"How do you build trust for a digital asset platform in a market exhausted by hype, burned by speculation, and deeply suspicious of anything that sounds like another app?"

“Real Metal, Real Ownership.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

SANU Coin is Nigeria's first asset-backed digital bullion platform — allowing users to buy, hold, and physically redeem gold and silver. The campaign needed to position SANU as a calm, credible alternative to speculative crypto and unstable savings apps.

The Challenge

Nigeria's consumer is living through persistent inflation anxiety and deep distrust of paper promises. Over-hyped financial products have made the average Nigerian investor defensive. The category tension: people understand gold culturally, but most digital finance advertising feels like speed, noise, and speculation. SANU needed to occupy the opposite space entirely.

My Approach

The strategic diagnosis: SANU must behave like financial infrastructure, not another app. I built the strategy around four pillars — Trust, Access, Tangibility, and Tone. OOH in Lagos served as a legitimacy layer. The ₦100 entry point reframed gold ownership from elite privilege to everyday sovereignty. A digital funnel moved audiences from awareness through education to conversion.

The Big Idea

Real Metal, Real Ownership. In a market tired of hype and paper promises, SANU positions bullion as the calm, credible alternative — financial infrastructure, not another app. Start from ₦100 and build real, sovereign-grade value in small, consistent actions. Buy digitally, redeem physically — so what you own is provable, tangible, and protected.

FMCG · Canvest Group

Integrated Campaign Strategy · Account Lead

Ducros Seasoning

"How does a brand return after two decades of advertising silence without sounding like an apology?"

“Seasoned With Time.”

View full strategy ↗
The Brief

Ducros — a heritage seasoning brand known for Thyme and Curry — had flavoured Nigerian kitchens for generations but went dark on advertising for nearly two decades. I served as strategist and account manager across influencer marketing, digital content, and OOH billboard advertising with a total campaign spend of ₦16M.

The Challenge

The category was intensely competitive. Gino had Hilda Baci. Knorr had a full creator programme. Maggi had the August Meeting. Terra had BBNaija. Ducros was returning with no recent brand presence, no ambassador, and no digital footprint — into a category where every major competitor was actively spending and winning cultural moments.

My Approach

The insight came from consumer research: urban Nigerians specifically seek out Ducros Curry for its fragrance, even at a premium. That loyalty is driven by sensory memory. The strategy repositioned the silence itself — not as something to apologise for, but as the quiet confidence of a brand that had been letting quality speak all along. Ducros did not go anywhere. It was always in the kitchen.

The Big Idea

Seasoned With Time. This idea bridges Ducros' heritage with its renewed relevance — celebrating the brand's enduring place in Nigerian kitchens. From grandma's pot to today's party rice, Ducros has seasoned our lives. It is not a comeback. It is a homecoming.

Beyond Strategy

What else I bring

◈
Brand Identity Design

Strong eye for visual identity — colour, typography, layout, and brand expression. Design is not separate from strategy for me; it is how strategy becomes felt.

◉
AI for Marketing

Prompt engineering, AI animation, and image generation using Claude, ChatGPT, Kimi, and Flow. Trained an entire agency team. Produced an original AI-animated series.

◎
Canva — Advanced

Agency-level decks, brand websites, and presentation systems. Three live Canva websites built. If it needs to look exceptional, I build it myself.

◆
Writing & Storytelling

Strategy documents, brand narratives, creative briefs, and content — all with the same commitment to clarity. The strongest ideas in my strategies always express in a single unforgettable line.

◇
Personal Brand Building

Built my personal brand from zero — ManyChat funnels, Mainstack and Selar products, 100K+ cumulative impressions. I teach it because I live it.

○
Training & Development

Led 15+ teams, trained 60+ young professionals. Designed QVT Youth Mentorship Initiative from scratch. Built Career Hill — a near-peer coaching model that gets graduates employed in weeks.

About

The mind behind it

I got into brand strategy through a rabbit hole that started with watching a designer named Abi Connick talk about how strategy made her work distinct. That observation — that thinking is what separates memorable brands from forgettable ones — has never left me.

My subsequent roles were not always called brand strategy. Communications. Social media. Media planning. Marketing management. But in every one of them, I applied brand strategy even when it was not asked of me. How a brand is positioned and perceived is upstream of everything else.

With six years of experience across FMCG, fintech, pharma, sports, fashion, and edtech — and a youth community I built from zero to over 1,000 people using nothing but brand thinking — I believe the right strategy does not just help a brand compete. It changes what competing means.
